news_summary: Today's space news highlights NASA's discovery of water signs on a unique asteroid, the collaborative spirit of the Expedition 73 crew in orbit, and Botswana joining the Artemis Accords to advance international space cooperation.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant la découverte par la NASA de traces d'eau sur un astéroïde unique, l'esprit de collaboration de l'équipage de l'Expédition 73 en orbite, ainsi que l'adhésion du Botswana aux Accords Artemis visant à renforcer la coopération spatiale internationale.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan el descubrimiento por parte de la NASA de indicios de agua en un asteroide único, el espíritu de colaboración de la tripulación de la Expedición 73 en órbita y la adhesión de Botsuana a los Acuerdos Artemis para impulsar la cooperación espacial internacional.

articles.title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
articles.summary: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.
articles.content:
Even relatively small asteroids can have surprisingly eventful histories. NASA's Lucy spacecraft recently revealed that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped object that has been shaped by collisions, sunlight, and even a brief encounter with liquid water long ago.

The asteroid formed about 155 million years ago when fragments from a violent collision gradually came together. Since then, a subtle but persistent force generated by sunlight has altered its rotation, while traces of ancient water remain preserved in its rocky surface.

On April 20, 2025, Lucy passed within 650 miles of Donaldjohanson while traveling through the main asteroid belt on its way to a group of Jupiter Trojan asteroids. During the flyby, the spacecraft captured the first close-up images and collected detailed scientific measurements. Those observations revealed an asteroid that does not rotate in the simple way scientists expected.

Lucy also provided detailed views of Donaldjohanson's unusual shape, along with craters, ridges, and other surface features that help tell the story of its evolution.

Lucy's Asteroid Flyby Reveals an Unusual Rotation

The encounter served as a practice run for Lucy's future visits to the Trojan asteroids, beginning with its flyby of Eurybates on Aug. 12, 2027. While testing spacecraft systems and mission operations, scientists gained a valuable opportunity to study a previously unexplored asteroid and compare it with Bennu and Ryugu, two asteroids that have been examined up close through sample-return missions.

Researchers reported their findings on June 18 in the journal Science.

Before Lucy's arrival, astronomers studying Donaldjohanson from Earth noticed a repeating pattern in the asteroid's brightness. Those observations suggested it was an elongated object completing one rotation every 10.5 Earth days.

The spacecraft's close-up measurements revealed a more complicated reality.

Instead of rotating around a single axis like most asteroids and planets, Donaldjohanson behaves more like a wobbling spinning top. Scientists found that it turns end-over-end once every 10.5 days while also rocking back and forth around its long axis once every 26.5 days.

Peanut-Shaped Asteroid Formed From Ancient Collision

Earth-based observations had already hinted that Donaldjohanson was elongated, but Lucy showed that the asteroid is actually composed of two connected lobes joined by a narrow neck.

Scientists describe this type of structure as bilobate. It likely formed when two fragments produced by an earlier collision drifted together and merged under their own gravity.

Researchers estimate that the asteroid was spinning at least ten times faster shortly after it formed. Over the last 20 to 60 million years, however, that rotation gradually slowed.

As the spin rate decreased, the balance between centrifugal forces and gravity changed. Loose rock and debris shifted down slopes, reshaping parts of the surface and contributing to the softened appearance of many craters visible in Lucy's images.

The team believes this slowdown was caused by the YORP effect, a subtle process driven by sunlight.

When sunlight warms an asteroid, the surface releases that energy as infrared radiation. Although the resulting recoil force is extremely small, it acts continuously over millions of years. Because Donaldjohanson's shape is uneven, those tiny forces do not cancel out completely and instead create a twisting effect that gradually alters the asteroid's rotation.

The same process can either slow or accelerate an asteroid's spin. Bennu, which rotates once every four hours, and Ryugu, which rotates roughly once every seven hours, were likely spinning much more slowly in the distant past before YORP sped them up.

Evidence of Ancient Water on Donaldjohanson

As Lucy raced past Donaldjohanson at about 30,000 mph, its instruments detected iron-rich clay minerals on the asteroid's surface.

These minerals could only have formed in the presence of liquid water. However, scientists believe the water exposure was relatively brief.

Over time, prolonged interaction with water tends to replace iron within clay minerals with other elements such as magnesium. Because the clays on Donaldjohanson remain iron-rich, researchers concluded that liquid water was present only for a limited period.

The situation appears different for Bennu and Ryugu. Both asteroids contain magnesium-rich clays, suggesting they experienced much longer periods of water exposure, possibly lasting millions of years while they were still parts of larger parent bodies.

Those differences may indicate that the parent asteroids formed at different times or in different regions of the solar system before eventually moving into the main asteroid belt.

Comparing Donaldjohanson, Bennu, and Ryugu

Scientists believe Donaldjohanson originated from the rocky remains of a larger asteroid rich in carbon and water that was shattered in a collision within the main asteroid belt.

Bennu and Ryugu likely formed through a similar process and in the same general region. Yet important differences set them apart.

Donaldjohanson is only about 155 million years old, making it much younger than Bennu and Ryugu, which are estimated to have formed 1 to 2 billion years ago.

Its orbital history is also different. Donaldjohanson has remained in the asteroid belt since its formation, while Bennu and Ryugu eventually migrated into near-Earth orbits that periodically bring them close to our planet. Those trajectories made them ideal destinations for sample-return missions.

"It's helpful for scientists to compare Donaldjohanson with asteroids like Bennu and Ryugu, which are seemingly similar asteroids, because every subtle difference is another clue to our origin story," said Simone Marchi, Lucy deputy principal investigator and lead author of the study at the Boulder, Colorado, office of the Southwest Research Institute.

"Once we start learning more about the Trojans, a completely different population of space rocks with very different histories, our understanding of solar system formation is destined to be challenged," said Marchi.

Named after the famous fossilized human ancestor discovered in Ethiopia in 1974, Lucy is on its way to become the first mission to explore Jupiter's Trojan asteroids. These ancient and relatively unchanged objects formed early in solar system history and may help scientists better understand how the planets formed and migrated before settling into their present-day positions.

About the Lucy Mission

Lucy's principal investigator is based at the Boulder, Colorado, office of the Southwest Research Institute, which is headquartered in San Antonio. NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, oversees mission management, systems engineering, and safety and mission assurance. Lockheed Martin Space in Littleton, Colorado, built the spacecraft.

Lucy is the 13th mission in NASA's Discovery Program. The program is managed by NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama, for the agency's Science Mission Directorate in Washington.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery by NASA's Lucy spacecraft revealing detailed characteristics of asteroid Donaldjohanson, including its unusual rotation, formation history, and evidence of ancient water. This contributes meaningful knowledge about the solar system's history, benefiting the broader scientific community and public understanding of space. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible advancement in space exploration.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft reveals a wobbling asteroid with signs of ancient water
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Lucy spacecraft is exploring asteroids in the main asteroid belt and on its way to study Jupiter’s Trojan asteroids. These space rocks hold clues about the early history of our solar system.

**What happened** – On April 20, 2025, Lucy flew close to an asteroid named Donaldjohanson, passing within 650 miles. The spacecraft took detailed pictures and measurements, discovering that the asteroid is peanut-shaped with two connected parts, and it wobbles as it spins. Scientists also found iron-rich clay minerals that suggest the asteroid had a short exposure to liquid water long ago.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because Donaldjohanson’s unusual spinning pattern and shape tell a story about how it formed from a collision about 155 million years ago. The asteroid’s rotation has slowed over time due to sunlight pushing on it in a small but steady way. Finding signs of ancient water helps scientists understand how water might have existed on small space rocks, which is key to learning about the building blocks of planets.

**What's next step** – Lucy will continue its journey to visit Jupiter’s Trojan asteroids starting in 2027. Scientists will use what they learned from Donaldjohanson to prepare for these visits and compare findings. Studying these different asteroids will help improve knowledge of how the solar system formed and changed over billions of years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Lucy mission revealed that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped rock with traces of ancient water, offering new insights into the solar system’s past.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Lucy de la NASA révèle un astéroïde qui oscille et porte des traces d’eau ancienne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La sonde Lucy de la NASA explore des astéroïdes dans la ceinture principale et se dirige vers les astéroïdes troyens de Jupiter. Ces corps célestes contiennent des indices sur l’histoire ancienne du système solaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 20 avril 2025, Lucy est passée à 650 miles de l’astéroïde Donaldjohanson. La sonde a pris des images détaillées et fait des mesures montrant que l’astéroïde a la forme d’un cacahuète avec deux parties reliées, et qu’il oscille en tournant. Des minéraux argileux riches en fer ont été détectés, indiquant que l’astéroïde a été exposé brièvement à de l’eau liquide il y a longtemps.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car le mouvement inhabituel et la forme de Donaldjohanson racontent son origine, née d’une collision il y a environ 155 millions d’années. Sa rotation a ralenti sous l’effet d’une faible poussée solaire. La présence d’eau ancienne aide à mieux comprendre comment l’eau a pu exister sur de petits corps, essentiel pour étudier la formation des planètes.

**Prochaine étape** – Lucy poursuivra son voyage pour visiter les astéroïdes troyens de Jupiter à partir de 2027. Les scientifiques utiliseront ces données pour préparer ces visites et comparer les résultats. Étudier ces astéroïdes différents améliorera la compréhension de la formation et de l’évolution du système solaire.

**En résumé** – La mission Lucy a montré que l’astéroïde Donaldjohanson est un corps en forme de cacahuète qui oscille et garde des traces d’eau ancienne, offrant de nouvelles clés sur l’histoire du système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Lucy de la NASA descubre un asteroide que se tambalea y muestra señales de agua antigua
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La nave Lucy de la NASA está explorando asteroides en el cinturón principal y se dirige a estudiar los asteroides troyanos de Júpiter. Estas rocas espaciales contienen pistas sobre la historia temprana del sistema solar.

**Qué pasó** – El 20 de abril de 2025, Lucy pasó a 650 millas del asteroide Donaldjohanson. La nave tomó imágenes y mediciones detalladas que mostraron que el asteroide tiene forma de maní con dos partes unidas y que se tambalea mientras gira. También detectaron minerales arcillosos ricos en hierro, lo que indica que el asteroide estuvo expuesto brevemente a agua líquida hace mucho tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque el movimiento inusual y la forma de Donaldjohanson cuentan su origen, formado por una colisión hace unos 155 millones de años. Su rotación se ha desacelerado por una pequeña pero constante fuerza del sol. Encontrar signos de agua antigua ayuda a entender cómo pudo existir el agua en rocas pequeñas, clave para estudiar la formación de planetas.

**Próximo paso** – Lucy continuará su viaje para visitar los asteroides troyanos de Júpiter a partir de 2027. Los científicos usarán esta información para preparar esas visitas y comparar resultados. Estudiar estos asteroides diferentes ayudará a mejorar el conocimiento sobre la formación y evolución del sistema solar.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La misión Lucy reveló que el asteroide Donaldjohanson es una roca con forma de maní que se tambalea y conserva señales de agua antigua, ofreciendo nuevas ideas sobre el pasado del sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently wobbling, peanut-shaped asteroid with two connected lobes and a narrow neck, surrounded by subtle glowing sunlight rays illustrating the YORP effect, floating serenely in space with soft earth tones and hints of rusty iron-rich clay textures on its surface, symbolizing ancient water’s fleeting presence.

articles.title: Expedition 73 Crew Reflects on Science, Teamwork, and Life in Orbit
articles.summary: On June 16, astronauts and cosmonauts gathered at Space Center Houston to share stories from their missions aboard the International Space Station and recognize the teamwork and people on the ground that made their missions possible.  The Expedition 73 Welcome Home Ceremony brought together members of NASA’s SpaceX Crew-10, Soyuz MS-27, and NASA’s SpaceX Crew-11 missions. […]
articles.content:
The Expedition 73 crew attends a debrief and awards ceremony at Space Center Houston’s IMAX theater on June 16, 2026. NASA/Luna Posadas Nava

On June 16, astronauts and cosmonauts gathered at Space Center Houston to share stories from their missions aboard the International Space Station and recognize the teamwork and people on the ground that made their missions possible.

The Expedition 73 Welcome Home Ceremony brought together members of NASA’s SpaceX Crew-10, Soyuz MS-27, and NASA’s SpaceX Crew-11 missions. During the event, the crews reflected on the science, partnerships, and international collaboration that defined their time in orbit.

Remarks were delivered by NASA’s Johnson Space Center Director Vanessa Wyche, Low Earth Orbit Program’s Deputy Manager for the International Space Station Dina Contella, Richard Jones with NASA’s commercial crew office, Flight Operations Director Norm Knight, Johnson Employee Relations Lead David Kelley, and Space Center Houston Chief Operating and Strategy Officer Keesha Bullock. Together, they recognized the accomplishments of the crews and the team members who helped make the expedition a success.

NASA’s Johnson Space Center Director Vanessa Wyche gives opening remarks at the crew debrief and awards ceremony. NASA/Luna Posadas Nava

Wyche welcomed the crews home and reflected on the accomplishments of Expedition 73.

“Together, these crews exemplified professionalism, resilience, and the spirit of international cooperation,” Wyche said. “Their work ensured the continued success of the International Space Station Program and demonstrated the strength of our multi-vehicle crew transportation strategy.”

During the expedition, all available docking ports were occupied simultaneously for the first time, with eight spacecraft attached to the station. The crew also supported visiting missions, including Axiom Mission 4, and multiple cargo deliveries while maintaining a full schedule of scientific investigations.

Crew members completed three spacewalks, installing hardware that supports future solar array upgrades and maintenance activities critical to station operations.

NASA astronaut Anne McClain is photographed near one of the International Space Station’s main solar arrays during a spacewalk to upgrade the orbital outpost’s power generation system and relocate a communications antenna on May 1, 2026. NASA

The ceremony also recognized the workforce whose dedication supported every aspect of Expedition 73, from mission planning and operations to research, training, and crew safety.

“You learned each other’s languages, and often, when we didn’t know the right answers, you partnered with us, and you would come up with the answers and help,” said International Space Station Program Deputy Manager Dina Contella. “You really helped make us successful.”

She noted that collaboration extended well beyond the crew in orbit, with teams across the program matching that dedication throughout the expedition.

Contella shared that Expedition 73 included six cargo missions, the inaugural flight of JAXA’s (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) HTV-X1 cargo spacecraft, and more than 37,000 pounds of supplies, equipment, and scientific investigations delivered to the space station.

She also thanked the Commercial Crew Program and Flight Operations teams for helping safely transport crews to and from the station and support mission operations.

More than 1,000 employees representing 40 teams received NASA Group Achievement Awards, while 23 individuals were honored with Superior Achievement Awards for their contributions to the mission.

NASA astronaut Zena Cardman and JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) astronaut Kimiya Yui receive the NASA Exceptional Bravery Honors at the crew debrief. From left: NASA astronaut Zena Cardman, Johnson Director Vanessa Wyche, Richard Jones with NASA’s commercial crew office, International Space Station Deputy Manager Dina Contella, and JAXA astronaut Kimiya Yui. NASA/Luna Posadas Nava

NASA astronaut Zena Cardman and JAXA astronaut Kimiya Yui received NASA Exceptional Bravery Honors for demonstrating exceptional courage, leadership, and composure during a medical event.

Flight Operations Director Norm Knight thanked the teams that supported Expedition 73 from the ground while recognizing the crews’ contributions throughout their missions. “You represent an astronaut corps that is the best of the best, epitomizing courage and teamwork,” Knight told the crew. “Every opportunity or challenge that came your way, you met with confidence and creativity.”

NASA astronaut Jonny Kim conducts an investigation to assess the effects of microgravity on bone marrow stem cells, including their ability to secrete proteins that form and dissolve bone. NASA

Crew members reflected on the station’s legacy as a platform for discovery, innovation, and international partnership after more than 25 years of continuous human presence in orbit.

Research conducted during Expedition 73 included investigations in human physiology, biology, materials science, pharmaceutical development, and technologies designed to benefit life on Earth and future exploration missions.

The crews also discussed research aboard the station that will help prepare NASA for future missions to the Moon and Mars, including advanced life-support systems and water recovery technologies.

NASA astronaut Nichole Ayers holds space botany hardware that supports the low Earth orbit Integrated Flori-culture Experiment (LIFE) investigation as she floats inside the space station’s cupola. The study examines how radiation and microgravity affect plant growth to support future exploration and improve crop production on Earth. NASA

Beyond science and operations, the crew built strong bonds during their months in orbit. They marked birthdays, holidays, and mission milestones together, often creating elaborate cakes from the limited ingredients available aboard the station.

NASA astronaut Anne McClain celebrates her birthday with a cake, gifts, and cards aboard the space station’s Unity module. NASA

Many crew members said their strongest memories centered on the people around them, and that trust and teamwork remained essential to mission success.

Viewing Earth from orbit provided the crew with a powerful reminder of humanity’s shared connection.

“When you look back at Earth, what we have in common is so much more important than what makes us different,” said McClain. “We’re all on this one planet. We’re all on the same crew.”

Awardees pose for a group photo from the Expedition 73 crew debrief and awards ceremony. NASA/Luna Posadas Nava

The evening concluded with the crew expressing gratitude to all those who supported their missions from launch through landing.

“It was overwhelming in the most wonderful way to step off that aircraft and see so many team members who had supported us and see family and friends for the first time,” said Cardman. “We are so grateful.”

View the list of award recipients here.

Watch the full Expedition 73 crew debrief and awards ceremony below.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the successful completion of Expedition 73 aboard the International Space Station, emphasizing international cooperation, scientific research benefiting Earth and future space exploration, and the dedication of a large workforce. The story has broad significance for humanity's presence in space and advances in science and technology.
articles.personality_title: Expedition 73 crew celebrates teamwork and science after successful mission in space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The International Space Station (ISS) has been a home for astronauts from different countries for over 25 years. Expedition 73 was one of the latest missions where crews lived and worked together in space, conducting important research and maintaining the station.

**What happened** – On June 16, 2026, astronauts and cosmonauts from Expedition 73 gathered at Space Center Houston. This event, called the Welcome Home Ceremony, brought together crews from NASA’s SpaceX Crew-10, Soyuz MS-27, and SpaceX Crew-11 missions. They shared stories about their time in orbit and honored the teamwork between people in space and those supporting from Earth. Several NASA leaders spoke, and many team members received awards for their hard work.

**Impact** – Expedition 73 was special because it showed strong international cooperation and teamwork. For the first time, all docking ports on the ISS were occupied at once, with eight spacecraft attached. The crew completed three spacewalks to upgrade the station’s solar power system and supported many cargo missions, including Japan’s new HTV-X1 supply ship. Their research covered biology, medicine, and technology that helps both Earth and future space trips to the Moon and Mars.

**What's next step** – The work done on Expedition 73 sets the stage for upcoming missions to explore deeper into space. Scientists will continue using the ISS to test new life-support systems and study how plants grow in space. The teams on the ground will keep working to safely send and bring back astronauts while supporting more scientific discoveries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Expedition 73 demonstrated how teamwork between international crews and ground teams advances science and prepares humans for future space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’expédition 73 célèbre la coopération et la science après une mission réussie dans l’espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Station spatiale internationale (ISS) accueille des astronautes de différents pays depuis plus de 25 ans. L’expédition 73 est l’une des dernières missions durant lesquelles les équipages ont vécu et travaillé ensemble dans l’espace, menant des recherches importantes et assurant la maintenance de la station.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 16 juin 2026, des astronautes et cosmonautes de l’expédition 73 se sont réunis au Space Center Houston. Lors de cette cérémonie de bienvenue, les équipages des missions SpaceX Crew-10, Soyouz MS-27 et SpaceX Crew-11 de la NASA ont partagé leurs expériences en orbite et rendu hommage à la coopération entre les équipes dans l’espace et celles au sol. Plusieurs responsables de la NASA ont pris la parole, et de nombreux membres de l’équipe ont reçu des récompenses pour leur travail.

**Impact** – L’expédition 73 est remarquable car elle a montré une forte coopération internationale et un excellent travail d’équipe. Pour la première fois, tous les ports d’amarrage de l’ISS étaient occupés simultanément, avec huit engins spatiaux attachés. L’équipage a réalisé trois sorties dans l’espace pour améliorer le système solaire de la station et a soutenu de nombreuses missions de ravitaillement, dont le nouveau cargo japonais HTV-X1. Leurs recherches ont porté sur la biologie, la médecine et la technologie, utiles à la fois pour la Terre et pour les futures missions vers la Lune et Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – Le travail accompli lors de l’expédition 73 prépare les futures missions d’exploration spatiale. Les scientifiques continueront à utiliser l’ISS pour tester de nouveaux systèmes de survie et étudier la croissance des plantes en apesanteur. Les équipes au sol poursuivront leur soutien pour envoyer et ramener les astronautes en toute sécurité tout en favorisant de nouvelles découvertes scientifiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’expédition 73 a montré comment la coopération entre équipages internationaux et équipes au sol fait progresser la science et prépare les humains à l’exploration spatiale future.
articles.personality_title_es: La expedición 73 celebra el trabajo en equipo y la ciencia tras una misión exitosa en el espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Estación Espacial Internacional (EEI) ha sido el hogar de astronautas de diferentes países durante más de 25 años. La expedición 73 fue una de las últimas misiones donde las tripulaciones vivieron y trabajaron juntas en el espacio, realizando investigaciones importantes y manteniendo la estación.

**Qué pasó** – El 16 de junio de 2026, astronautas y cosmonautas de la expedición 73 se reunieron en el Space Center Houston. En esta ceremonia de bienvenida, las tripulaciones de las misiones SpaceX Crew-10, Soyuz MS-27 y SpaceX Crew-11 de la NASA compartieron sus historias en órbita y reconocieron el trabajo en equipo entre las personas en el espacio y quienes los apoyaron desde la Tierra. Varios líderes de la NASA dieron discursos y muchos miembros del equipo recibieron premios por su dedicación.

**Impacto** – La expedición 73 fue especial porque mostró una fuerte cooperación internacional y trabajo en equipo. Por primera vez, todos los puertos de atraque de la EEI estuvieron ocupados al mismo tiempo, con ocho naves espaciales acopladas. La tripulación realizó tres caminatas espaciales para mejorar el sistema solar de la estación y apoyó varias misiones de carga, incluyendo la nueva nave de suministro japonesa HTV-X1. Sus investigaciones abarcaron biología, medicina y tecnología que ayudan tanto a la Tierra como a futuros viajes espaciales a la Luna y Marte.

**Próximo paso** – El trabajo realizado en la expedición 73 prepara el camino para futuras misiones de exploración espacial. Los científicos seguirán usando la EEI para probar nuevos sistemas de soporte vital y estudiar el crecimiento de plantas en el espacio. Los equipos en tierra continuarán trabajando para enviar y traer astronautas con seguridad mientras apoyan nuevos descubrimientos científicos.

**Resumen en una frase** – La expedición 73 demostró cómo el trabajo en equipo entre tripulaciones internacionales y equipos en tierra impulsa la ciencia y prepara a la humanidad para la exploración espacial futura.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant space station orbiting Earth, surrounded by eight gently glowing spacecraft docked around it, with interconnected hands forming a circle around the station symbolizing international teamwork and collaboration, all rendered in soft natural tones of blue, white, and gentle metallic hues.

articles.title: NASA Welcomes Botswana as 68th Artemis Accords Signatory
articles.summary: The Republic of Botswana signed the Artemis Accords Thursday during a ceremony hosted by NASA at the agency’s headquarters in Washington, becoming the sixth African nation to join a growing community of nations committed to the peaceful, transparent, and responsible exploration of space. “It is my privilege to welcome Botswana as the newest signatory of the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Deputy […]
articles.content:
From left to right, Senior Advisor on Space for the Bureau of Oceans and International Environmental and Scientific Affairs Greg Autry, NASA Deputy Administrator Matt Anderson, Minister of Communications and Innovation David Tshere, and Acting Ambassador of the Republic of Botswana to the United States Mabedi Ngwenya pose for a photo following an Artemis Accords signing ceremony with the Republic of Botswana Thursday, June 25, 2026, at the Mary W. Jackson NASA Headquarters building in Washington. NASA/Keegan Barber

The Republic of Botswana signed the Artemis Accords Thursday during a ceremony hosted by NASA at the agency’s headquarters in Washington, becoming the sixth African nation to join a growing community of nations committed to the peaceful, transparent, and responsible exploration of space.

“It is my privilege to welcome Botswana as the newest signatory of the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Deputy Administrator Matt Anderson. “Today marks an important milestone in our international partnership and in the continued growth of the Artemis community. Botswana joins at an important moment. Earlier this month, we announced the crew of Artemis III and, as we speak, their spacecraft is being assembled as they prepare to play their part in mankind’s greatest adventure.”

Botswana’s Minister of Communications and Innovation David Tshere signed on behalf of the country. U.S. Department of State Senior Advisor for Space Gregory Autry, and Mabedi Ngwenya, acting ambassador of the Republic of Botswana to the United States, also participated in the ceremony.

“Botswana like many countries, we have interest in space exploration, found it important to become a signatory to the Artemis Accords to promote the safe, transparent, and sustainable civil space exploration, and to advance international cooperation, and a shared framework for responsible activities in the space,” said Tshere.

This new chapter builds on Botswana’s long history of collaboration with the United States in space-based Earth observation. In the early 1970s, Botswana participated in the satellite program later known as Landsat, joining dozens of other nations in pioneering satellite-based environmental observation. Botswana marked another milestone with the launch of its first Earth observation satellite, Botswana Satellite 1, in March 2025, aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9.

In 2020, during the first Trump Administration, the United States, led by NASA and the State Department, joined with seven other founding nations to establish the Artemis Accords, responding to the growing interest in lunar activities by both governments and private companies. The Artemis Accords introduced the first set of practical principles aimed at enhancing the safety and coordination between like-minded nations as they explore the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

Signing the Artemis Accords means committing to explore peaceably and transparently, to render aid to those in need, to enable access to scientific data that all of humanity can learn from, to ensure activities do not interfere with those of others, and to preserve historically significant sites and artifacts by developing best practices for space exploration for the benefit of all.

More countries are expected to sign the Artemis Accords in the months and years ahead, as NASA continues its work to establish a safe, peaceful, and prosperous future in space.

Learn more about the Artemis Accords at:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-accords
articles.reasoning: Botswana's signing of the Artemis Accords represents a meaningful international commitment to peaceful, transparent, and responsible space exploration, expanding global cooperation in space activities with broad significance beyond a single nation or niche group. The article provides detailed context about Botswana's history in space collaboration and the principles of the Artemis Accords, highlighting a positive step toward a safe and cooperative future in space exploration.
articles.personality_title: Botswana joins international space partnership through Artemis Accords
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Artemis Accords are agreements between countries to explore space peacefully and responsibly. They were first created in 2020 by NASA and the U.S. State Department to guide how nations work together on missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

**What happened** – On June 25, 2026, Botswana officially signed the Artemis Accords during a ceremony at NASA’s headquarters in Washington, D.C. This made Botswana the 68th country and the sixth in Africa to join this space exploration agreement. Botswana’s Minister of Communications and Innovation, David Tshere, signed the agreement, with NASA officials and Botswana’s acting ambassador present.

**Impact** – Botswana’s signing shows the country’s growing role in space activities. It has a history of working with the U.S. on satellite projects, including launching its own Earth observation satellite in 2025. By joining the Artemis Accords, Botswana commits to exploring space peacefully and sharing scientific information openly. This helps build trust and cooperation between nations as space missions become more common.

**What’s next step** – More countries are expected to join the Artemis Accords in the future. NASA will continue to lead international efforts to ensure space exploration is safe, fair, and benefits everyone. Botswana’s participation may also lead to more space projects and partnerships for the country.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Botswana’s joining the Artemis Accords marks an important step in global cooperation for responsible and peaceful space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Botswana rejoint l'accord international Artemis pour l'exploration spatiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les accords Artemis sont des engagements entre pays pour explorer l'espace de manière pacifique et responsable. Ils ont été créés en 2020 par la NASA et le département d'État américain pour guider la coopération sur les missions vers la Lune, Mars et au-delà.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 25 juin 2026, le Botswana a officiellement signé les accords Artemis lors d'une cérémonie au siège de la NASA à Washington, D.C. Le Botswana est devenu le 68e pays et le sixième en Afrique à rejoindre cet accord. Le ministre botswanais des Communications et de l'Innovation, David Tshere, a signé l'accord en présence des représentants de la NASA et de l'ambassadeur par intérim du Botswana.

**Impact** – Cette signature montre le rôle croissant du Botswana dans les activités spatiales. Le pays a déjà collaboré avec les États-Unis sur des satellites et a lancé son propre satellite d'observation de la Terre en 2025. En rejoignant les accords Artemis, le Botswana s'engage à explorer l'espace pacifiquement et à partager les données scientifiques, renforçant ainsi la confiance et la coopération entre nations.

**Prochaine étape** – D'autres pays devraient rejoindre les accords Artemis dans les mois et années à venir. La NASA continuera de diriger les efforts internationaux pour que l'exploration spatiale soit sûre, équitable et bénéfique pour tous. La participation du Botswana pourrait aussi ouvrir la voie à de nouveaux projets spatiaux pour le pays.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'adhésion du Botswana aux accords Artemis est une étape importante pour la coopération mondiale dans l'exploration spatiale responsable et pacifique.
articles.personality_title_es: Botsuana se une al acuerdo internacional Artemis para la exploración espacial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Acuerdos Artemis son compromisos entre países para explorar el espacio de manera pacífica y responsable. Fueron creados en 2020 por la NASA y el Departamento de Estado de EE.UU. para guiar la cooperación en misiones a la Luna, Marte y más allá.

**Qué pasó** – El 25 de junio de 2026, Botsuana firmó oficialmente los Acuerdos Artemis en una ceremonia en la sede de la NASA en Washington, D.C. Botsuana se convirtió en el país número 68 y el sexto en África en unirse a este acuerdo. El ministro de Comunicaciones e Innovación, David Tshere, firmó el acuerdo con la presencia de funcionarios de la NASA y el embajador interino de Botsuana.

**Impacto** – La firma muestra el papel creciente de Botsuana en actividades espaciales. El país ha trabajado con EE.UU. en proyectos de satélites y lanzó su propio satélite de observación terrestre en 2025. Al unirse a los Acuerdos Artemis, Botsuana se compromete a explorar el espacio pacíficamente y a compartir datos científicos, fortaleciendo la confianza y cooperación entre países.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que más países se unan a los Acuerdos Artemis en el futuro. La NASA seguirá liderando esfuerzos internacionales para que la exploración espacial sea segura, justa y beneficiosa para todos. La participación de Botsuana podría abrir nuevas oportunidades espaciales para el país.

**Resumen en una frase** – La adhesión de Botsuana a los Acuerdos Artemis es un paso clave para la cooperación global en la exploración espacial responsable y pacífica.
